item.title: Most people stop at Pi-hole, but Unbound is the privacy upgrade your home network actually needs
item.itunes:title: Most people stop at Pi-hole, but Unbound is the privacy upgrade your home network actually needs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Pi-hole is one of the best upgrades you can make to your home network if you have a device capable of self-hosting it. There are other alternatives, of course, like AdGuard Home, but Pi-hole has a very easy-to-understand UI that makes it one of the better options out there.

However, Pi-hole alone isn't the only solution for making your network's DNS queries more secure and private. If you want your network privacy to be taken to the next level, Unbound DNS is the way to do it, and pairing it with Pi-hole is the perfect setup to balance ease of use and privacy.

Pi-hole is great for blocking ads (and more)

But it still needs a DNS server

Pi-hole is a tool for handling DNS queries, and it's perfect for the purpose of blocking or filtering certain requests. You can use it to help monitor requests within your network so you know where traffic is going, plus you can block some of those requests, such as a smart home device that likes to phone home a little too often, or simply blocking access to adult sites.

Of course, what a lot of people use it for is blocking ads. You can add known ad servers to block lists in Pi-hole and make it so your entire network is generally free of ads on the internet, aside from places like YouTube, where video ads are immune to this. Popular blocklists already exist online to make this easier, so you can just import them and be on your way.

Tailscale plus Pi-hole gives you DNS control that Apple and Google will never allow

Reclaim your DNS privacy.

2
By  Samir Makwana

But Pi-hole doesn't actually resolve any of your DNS queries itself. It checks against your filters and drops any blocked connections, and then it connects to an upstream DNS resolver server, such as Google, to actually determine what IP a specific address is associated with. That means your DNS requests are all still logged through Google, or whatever external resolver you're using, which means your connections to any website are still known by a third-party.

Unbound adds a layer of privacy

Who needs Google anyway?

This is where Unbound DNS comes in. Unbound is a "local, validating, and recursive" DNS resolver, meaning it uses a built-in list of root nameservers that first solve the domains for the root hints (such as .net or .com) which then respond with a top-level domain that can then determine which authoritative servers are handling the specific domain you're looking for, and only after that does it ask that authoritative name server for the IP associated with that domain.

This recursive approach abstracts your DNS queries so that your requests don't all go through a single server and log your internet activity as a whole. Instead of using Google to resolve every DNS query, Google will only know you made one when you're specifically querying a Google website.

This does come with the downside that your initial request to a website will take longer because there are multiple requests that need to be made until you get to the actual IP address. However, subsequent queries to the same top-level domain are resolved much more quickly anyway.

5 reasons I run my own DNS server with Unbound

Having a local DNS resolver brings many benefits.

5
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Unbound has plenty of capabilities that overlap with those of Pi-hole, but the latter has a much more friendly interface for seeing your DNS query logs, setting up blocklists, and so on. Your best bet for pairing them together is to simply set Unbound as your upstream DNS resolver in Pi-hole. That way, Pi-hole still handles all the filtering of unwanted domains and provides a nice dashboard for visualizing your queries, while Unbound handles resolving the DNS queries that do need to leave your network in a much more private way compared to using well-known public DNS servers, but you don't need to interact with it on a regular basis.

Setting it up is relatively easy

For self-hosting standards, that is

Running Pi-hole alongside Unbound may depend on your setup. Some routers, especially OPNsense ones, make it easy to set up Unbound, but I don't have one of those routers. I do have Pi-hole running in a TrueNAS VM inside Proxmox, so initially I thought I'd set up Unbound in TrueNAS as well, but there's no easy-to-install Unbound app on the marketplace. So then I shifted to Proxmox itself.

There is a Proxmox helper script for setting up an Unbound LXC, but I had some issues getting this running, so what I did instead was spin up a plain Debian LXC and install Unbound on it using the standard package manager. I followed the official Unbound guide for basic configuration of Unbound itself, and I also added a Firewall rule to the LXC in Proxmox so that it would accept incoming DNS requests.

Then it was a simple matter of pointing my router's DNS server to my Pi-hole instance, and then point Pi-hole to use my Debian LXC as an upstream DNS resolver. I have to admit I was almost surprised when I saw everything working properly, but it was a magical moment. Through the Pi-hole logs, I was able to verify that queries were being forwarded to the LXC running Unbound, and by enabling Unbound logging, I was also able to see it receiving requests in real time. Figuring all this out in real time to write this article had its fair share of frustrations, but it was actually easier to set up than I expected.

4 reasons you should run your own DNS server with Unbound

Upgrading your network with a self-hosted DNS server is one of the best improvements you can make

4
By  Elliot Alexander

Pi-hole and Unbound make for a fantastic combo

Self-hosting has always been about taking more control over your data and privacy, and whereas Pi-hole already goes a long way by blocking websites and servers that deliver ads or other unwanted content, Unbound is a privacy upgrade that's certainly worth considering. It makes your connection to the internet more private from the very start, since even just resolving a URL is done in a more private way instead of relying on centralized servers. Of course, if you want to set this up for maximum reliability, you might want multiple instances of Unbound running, just as you might want multiple Pi-holes, but the basic principle is always the same.

Pi-hole
OS
Linux
Price model
Free
See at Github
Unbound
OS
Windows, Linux, macOS
Price model
Free
See at NLnet labs

However, Pi-hole alone isn't the only solution for making your network's DNS queries more secure and private. If you want your network privacy to be taken to the next level, Unbound DNS is the way to do it, and pairing it with Pi-hole is the perfect setup to balance ease of use and privacy.

You can use it to help monitor requests within your network so you know where traffic is going, plus you can block some of those requests, such as a smart home device that likes to phone home a little too often, or simply blocking access to adult sites.

Of course, what a lot of people use it for is blocking ads. You can add known ad servers to block lists in Pi-hole and make it so your entire network is generally free of ads on the internet, aside from places like YouTube, where video ads are immune to this. For self-hosting standards, that is

I do have Pi-hole running in a TrueNAS VM inside Proxmox, so initially I thought I'd set up Unbound in TrueNAS as well, but there's no easy-to-install Unbound app on the marketplace. So then I shifted to Proxmox itself.

There is a Proxmox helper script for setting up an Unbound LXC, but I had some issues getting this running, so what I did instead was spin up a plain Debian LXC and install Unbound on it using the standard package manager. I followed the official Unbound guide for basic configuration of Unbound itself, and I also added a Firewall rule to the LXC in Proxmox so that it would accept incoming DNS requests.

Then it was a simple matter of pointing my router's DNS server to my Pi-hole instance, and then point Pi-hole to use my Debian LXC as an upstream DNS resolver. I have to admit I was almost surprised when I saw everything working properly, but it was a magical moment. Through the Pi-hole logs, I was able to verify that queries were being forwarded to the LXC running Unbound, and by enabling Unbound logging, I was also able to see it receiving requests in real time. Figuring all this out in real time to write this article had its fair share of frustrations, but it was actually easier to set up than I expected.

4 reasons you should run your own DNS server with Unbound

Upgrading your network with a self-hosted DNS server is one of the best improvements you can make

4
By  Elliot Alexander

item.title: DriftGuard aims to make gamepad strick drift a thing of the past
item.itunes:title: DriftGuard aims to make gamepad strick drift a thing of the past
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • DriftGuard allows you to reprogram any Xbox controller's joysticks, eliminating strick drift as long as it's not a hardware related issue.
  • The app's creator says the fix is unpatchable and permanent, which means Microsoft won't be able to block it.
  • It's unclear if the calibration features will eventually work with PlayStation DualSense and Switch gamepads.

If you're a gamer, it's likely you've encountered Xbox gamepad stick drift at some point. Maybe you were playing Halo 3 on the Xbox 360 back in the day, only to find the Master Chief was suddenly leaning to the left a bit, resulting in you getting popped by a suicidal, plasma grenade-wielding grunt? Perhaps more recently, your car in Forza Horizon 6 suddenly started veering to the right?

Usually, this means you need to make a warranty claim with Microsoft if you're lucky enough that the controller is a somewhat recent purchase, which forces you to send the gamepad off for repairs. If it's an older controller, your only choice is to buy a new gamepad. Even if you're more technically savvy and pop open the gamepad to replace the sticks, you likely won't be able to completely eliminate the issue.

This is where DriftGuard comes in. Its recent app update aims to address Xbox controller stick drift without requiring a hardware fix. According to @Modyfikator89 on Twitter, a controller repair specialist, DriftGuard is a "historic hardware discovery" and has unlocked "manual & automatic joystick calibration for any Xbox Controller." The app's creator says the solution is unpatchable and permanent, meaning Microsoft won't be able to update its gamepads to break DriftGuard compatibility.

If the Xbox controller has a more serious hardware issue, DriftGuard won't fix its stick drift

The app is compatible with pretty much every official Xbox gamepad

According to @Modyfikator89, DriftGuard works with Xbox 360, Xbox One, Xbox One X/S, the Xbox Elite Series 1/2, and Scuff Instinct Pro controllers, across both wired and wireless variants. Along with Xbox gamepads, DriftGuard also supports PlayStation DualSense and Nintendo Switch controllers, but the new stick drift calibration feature has only been tested to work with Xbox gamepads. I haven't personally tried DriveGuard yet, but early impressions online seem to be positive. While there's an Android DriftGuard app, it also works directly in any web browser.

It's important to note that while DriftGuard can reprogram an Xbox controller's movement when the stick is stationary by assigning a new neutral position, effectively eliminating stick drift, if the controller's joystick sensors are damaged or there's another, more serious hardware issue, the gamepad will still suffer from stick drift. Additionally, stick drift can be caused by other factors, including worn contacts and even dust, which DriftGuard obviously also can't fix.

Deals

Save on Gaming Gear: Controller Deals & Discounts

Find gaming gear deals to save on controllers, replacement thumbsticks, charging docks, and repair kits. Browse discounts and limited-time offers on headsets, pads, and accessories in the Gaming Gear category to replace, upgrade, or protect your setup aff
Deals Explore Gaming Gear Deals

Microsoft has an official calibration tool in its Xbox Accessories app, but it only works for the Xbox Elite Wireless Controller Series 1/2 (I've also found that it doesn't work very well). Right now, DriftGuard is free (the Steam version of the app hasn't been updated with the new stick drift-fixing feature yet).

Someone turned their ESP32 CYD into an adorable ASCII aquarium, and so can you

It's a lot cheaper to maintainer than a real aquarium.

By  Simon Batt
item.description:
Summary

  • DriftGuard allows you to reprogram any Xbox controller's joysticks, eliminating strick drift as long as it's not a hardware related issue.
  • The app's creator says the fix is unpatchable and permanent, which means Microsoft won't be able to block it.
  • It's unclear if the calibration features will eventually work with PlayStation DualSense and Switch gamepads.

If you're a gamer, it's likely you've encountered Xbox gamepad stick drift at some point. Maybe you were playing Halo 3 on the Xbox 360 back in the day, only to find the Master Chief was suddenly leaning to the left a bit, resulting in you getting popped by a suicidal, plasma grenade-wielding grunt? Perhaps more recently, your car in Forza Horizon 6 suddenly started veering to the right?

Usually, this means you need to make a warranty claim with Microsoft if you're lucky enough that the controller is a somewhat recent purchase, which forces you to send the gamepad off for repairs. If it's an older controller, your only choice is to buy a new gamepad. Even if you're more technically savvy and pop open the gamepad to replace the sticks, you likely won't be able to completely eliminate the issue.

This is where DriftGuard comes in. Its recent app update aims to address Xbox controller stick drift without requiring a hardware fix. According to @Modyfikator89 on Twitter, a controller repair specialist, DriftGuard is a "historic hardware discovery" and has unlocked "manual & automatic joystick calibration for any Xbox Controller." The app's creator says the solution is unpatchable and permanent, meaning Microsoft won't be able to update its gamepads to break DriftGuard compatibility.

If the Xbox controller has a more serious hardware issue, DriftGuard won't fix its stick drift

The app is compatible with pretty much every official Xbox gamepad

According to @Modyfikator89, DriftGuard works with Xbox 360, Xbox One, Xbox One X/S, the Xbox Elite Series 1/2, and Scuff Instinct Pro controllers, across both wired and wireless variants. Along with Xbox gamepads, DriftGuard also supports PlayStation DualSense and Nintendo Switch controllers, but the new stick drift calibration feature has only been tested to work with Xbox gamepads. I haven't personally tried DriveGuard yet, but early impressions online seem to be positive. While there's an Android DriftGuard app, it also works directly in any web browser.

It's important to note that while DriftGuard can reprogram an Xbox controller's movement when the stick is stationary by assigning a new neutral position, effectively eliminating stick drift, if the controller's joystick sensors are damaged or there's another, more serious hardware issue, the gamepad will still suffer from stick drift. Additionally, stick drift can be caused by other factors, including worn contacts and even dust, which DriftGuard obviously also can't fix.
